result:
I keep thinking about the hour just after the streetlights come on, when the sky is still bruised-purple overhead but the pavement already belongs to the small conspiracies of night.  That hour has a hush to it, a hush that feels like it was minted just this once, never to be spent again.  I used to believe the hush was waiting for me personally, that it folded its wings only when I stepped outside, but I’ve lived long enough to know the arrogance of that.  Still, arrogance and wonder are cousins, and I keep showing up at the same address to see which one opens the door.

Tonight it’s wonder.  The air smells of warm dryer lint from somebody’s basement vent and the greener, peppery scent of maples exhaling the day’s heat.  A neighbor’s sprinkler goes ts-ts-ts, then ka-chunk, as it ratchets through its arc, and each wet pulse lands on the sidewalk like a sentence I almost understand.  I’m walking barefoot because I discovered, at forty-one, that if you walk barefoot late enough the asphalt remembers the noon sun and gives it back to you in slow syllables.  It’s a conversation: sole to soul, cheesy but accurate.  The grit sticks to me—mulch grains, a foil gum wrapper, the spine of a maple leaf—but I don’t mind.  I’m collecting evidence that I was here, that the day didn’t simply pass through me like weather through a screen.

I read once that every person you’ve ever met occupies a neuronal village in your head, and if you live long enough the population starts to feel like an inner metropolis.  Tonight the city council is in session.  My mother sits on a folding chair in the front row, wearing the burgundy sweater she died in, patiently waiting to ask why I still haven’t repotted the African violet she left on the windowsill.  Next to her is the eighth-grade teacher who told me metaphors were “tired fireworks,” and whose name, absurdly, was Mr. Firestone.  Behind them, the barista who drew constellations in my latte foam last March, insisting Orion’s belt could steer me toward a better version of myself.  They all mutter, cross-talk, pass notes.  The mayor gavels for order, but the mayor is me at seven, wearing a cape made from a stained bath towel, and I never had much authority.

What they want, I think, is revision.  They want me to edit the past until it stops itching.  But revision is a bottomless well: lean too far and you tumble in, scrabbling at slick stones, never finding the original version again.  So I keep walking, letting the cicadas crank their tiny rotary phones.  Two blocks over, the Methodist church has wedged its marquee between the sins of the flesh and the promise of air-conditioning.  This week it says: “If you’re waiting for a sign, this is it.”  The letters sag a little, as if even the plastic feels the weight of such certainty.  I photograph it anyway, because I collect unfinished sermons the way other people collect ticket stubs.  They remind me that language, like bread, goes stale unless it’s broken and shared.

At the corner where the elm was removed last spring, the stump has been planed smooth and varnished by rain.  Someone has crayoned a sunburst onto the grain, orange and yellow halos radiating outward.  The tree’s absence is a throat-clearing: notice, notice.  I sit on it, an impromptu ottoman, and feel the latent throb of xylem and phlofem, like a phantom limb.  I want to tell the crayon artist that the rings inside this trunk recorded the year my parents met, the year I learned to ride a bike, the summer nobody could sleep for heat.  But art doesn’t need footnotes; it needs witnesses.  I witness.  Orange smears under my palms when I stand up, and for a second I’m wearing the tree’s sunset, a temporary tattoo.

Across the street, a porch light flicks on and a man in a white undershirt steps out with a dog older than debt.  The dog moves like a furry metronome, each step a half-note slower than the last.  The man waits without checking his phone, a patience so complete it looks like reverence.  I want to cross and ask him what he knows about time that I don’t, but the spell feels fragile, so I stay on my curb, an acolyte at a distance.  The dog lifts its leg against the same mailbox post it has anointed for a decade, and the man murmurs “Good job, good job,” as if the dog has just solved an equation.  Perhaps it has: measure the perimeter of your territory nightly, reaffirm that what was yours is still yours, return inside for a cookie.  The formula fits.

I resume walking, past houses hoarding blue television snow behind half-shut blinds.  Inside, people are cheering strangers who bake cakes under impossible deadlines, or documenting the mating habits of deep-sea mollusks.  I imagine their living rooms smelling of microwaved popcorn and Febreeze, and I feel the familiar ache of wanting to be invited everywhere, simultaneously, while also wanting to remain this unobserved, barefoot nomad smelling of dryer lint and crayon wax.  The contradiction doesn’t resolve; it just keeps walking with me, like a second shadow that’s learned my gait.

The Episcopal church a block north has installed a labyrinth in what used to be a side yard of stubborn hostas.  It’s a simple thing: river stones mortared into looping turns that pretend to meander but actually calculate their way to the center like a Fibonacci spiral wearing humility as a disguise.  Tonight the labyrinth is empty, lit only by a motion-sensing flood that clicks on when I approach, as if to say, “Ah, the next pilgrim.”  I toe the entrance, remembering the instructions from the laminated sheet that usually sits in a plexiglass box: Release, Receive, Return.  Three verbs for the price of one eternal question.

I begin.  The stones are cool now, their day’s heat spent.  Left turn, right, double back—each pivot a stanza break.  I think about the word “labyrinth” versus “maze.”  A maze is engineered confusion; a labyrinth is engineered confession.  You can’t get lost here, only exposed.  Halfway in, the floodlight times out and darkness reclaims the yard.  I keep moving by starlight, trusting the pattern the way one trusts the beating of an unfamiliar but insistent drum.  At the center I find a single plastic dinosaur, a stegosaurus, its spine encrusted with glitter.  Someone’s joke, someone’s relic.  I pick it up; the glitter sheds like frost.  Here is everything I needed to be told: that the journey deposits us in jurisdiction of the absurd, that we leave offerings for strangers, that even plastic can be luminous if handled gently enough.

I pocket the dinosaur and retrace the loops outward.  The floodlight wakes again, surprised to see me leaving already.  It clicks off a second time just as I step onto the grass, and the hush that returns feels freshly minted, a coin pressed into my palm.  I walk home carrying it, past the sprinkler now silenced, past the stump that will still glow faintly tomorrow under the morning sun, past the dog and the man long gone inside.  My soles are black with asphalt, my pocket glitter-shedding, my neuronal city momentarily quiet, appeased by the simple fact of motion.

Tomorrow the headlines will screech, the microwave will beep, the emails will stack like unread psalms.  But tonight I have walked a spiral laid by human hands, palmed a dinosaur older than my memories yet brand-new, and let the maples breathe their green secrets against my skin.  The hour has done what it always does: it ended.  Yet something persists, a filament of orange crayon beneath a fingernail, a fossil heat in the arch of my foot, a hush folded small enough to fit behind the ear, waiting for the next dark to unfurl.  That, too, is a kind of sign, though I’m no longer waiting for permission to read it.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
